1.pericope_label: The Song of Moses and the Lamb
1.synthesis: After the harvest of the earth in chapter 14, John sees 'another sign in heaven, great and amazing, seven angels with seven plagues, which are the last, for with them the wrath of God is finished' (v.1). Before the bowls are poured, the scene pauses on a vision of the redeemed: those who 'had conquered the beast and its image and the number of its name' stand beside a sea of glass mingled with fire, holding harps of God (v.2). They sing 'the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb' (v.3). The integrative thread is deliberate: the most terrible judgment series in the book opens not with the suffering of the wicked but with the worship of the victorious. Heaven interprets the plagues before earth feels them, and the interpretation is doxology — 'Great and amazing are your deeds, O Lord God the Almighty! Just and true are your ways, O King of the nations!' (v.3).
1.historical_context: The 'song of Moses' recalls the hymn sung at the Red Sea after Israel passed through on dry ground and watched the Egyptian army drown (Exodus 15); a second song of Moses, the covenant witness of Deuteronomy 32, vindicates God's justice against the nations. John's churches, pressed by the imperial cult under Domitian (c. AD 95), would hear in this a promise: as Pharaoh's tyranny could not finally hold God's people, neither can Rome's. The 'sea of glass mingled with fire' (v.2) blends the calm crystal sea before the throne (Revelation 4:6) with the fire of judgment now imminent. Victorinus of Pettau and Bede, the earliest commentators preserved here, read the sea as the font of baptism and the conquerors as the Church standing firm on its confession — the persecuted reading their own perseverance into the vision.
1.literary_structure: This is the third of the book's great heavenly interludes that precede a judgment cycle (compare the throne-room of Revelation 4–5 before the seals, and the sealing of Revelation 7 before the trumpets). The sevenfold pattern — seals, trumpets, bowls — intensifies each time, and the bowls are explicitly 'the last,' completing (Greek etelesthē, 'finished') the wrath. The number seven signals completeness; the fathers note that the wrath that 'smites with seven plagues' echoes the sevenfold covenant curse of Leviticus 26. The song's couplets are a mosaic of Old Testament praise (Psalm 86:9–10; Psalm 111:2; Jeremiah 10:7), so that the new song is sung in the old covenant's own words.
1.theological_application: The passage frames divine wrath inside divine justice. The conquerors do not gloat over the plagues; they confess that God's 'ways' are 'just and true' and that 'all nations will come and worship' before him (v.4). Judgment in Revelation is never arbitrary cruelty but the moral order of a holy God asserting itself, and even it serves the larger purpose of the nations' worship. For the suffering church the application is endurance: to 'conquer' the beast is not to escape death but to refuse its mark and keep faith, and the reward is to stand in worship on the far shore. The vision also disciplines our speech about wrath — the only fitting response to God's judgments is the song, not speculation.
1.christology: The redeemed sing 'the song of Moses... and the song of the Lamb' (v.3) — one song, two names, because the deliverance Moses prefigured is accomplished by Christ. The exodus through the sea finds its antitype in the Lamb's blood, by which the conquerors overcame (Revelation 12:11). That the nations will worship the 'King of the nations' fulfills the promise that in Abraham's seed all nations would be blessed (Genesis 12:3), and anticipates the throne shared by God and the Lamb in the new creation (Revelation 22:3). The terror of the bowls is bracketed by the Lamb who was slain, so that judgment is read in the light of the cross.

5.synthesis: The worship ends and the temple opens: 'After this I looked, and the sanctuary of the tent of witness in heaven was opened' (v.5). Out come the seven angels with the seven plagues, robed in pure bright linen with golden sashes, and one of the four living creatures gives them 'seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God who lives forever and ever' (v.7). Then 'the sanctuary was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power, and no one could enter the sanctuary until the seven plagues of the seven angels were finished' (v.8). The through-line is the heavenly authorization of the bowls: they issue from God's own dwelling, handed over by the worshipping creatures, so that what earth will experience as catastrophe is shown to originate in the holiness of God.
5.historical_context: 'The tent of witness' (Greek skēnē tou martyriou) is the Septuagint's name for the wilderness tabernacle that housed the tablets of the testimony (Exodus 38:21; Numbers 1:50). John's hearers, formed by synagogue reading, would catch that the plagues come from the same sanctuary that held God's covenant law — the law Rome and the beast-worshippers have flouted. The 'golden bowls' (Greek phialai) are the shallow temple basins used to pour out drink offerings and to carry incense (Revelation 5:8); now they pour not incense but wrath. The angels' priestly linen and golden sashes mark them as ministers of the sanctuary, executing a liturgical act, not a chaotic rampage.
5.literary_structure: The opened sanctuary recalls Revelation 11:19, where the heavenly temple was opened and the ark appeared, knitting the bowl cycle to what precedes. The smoke that fills the temple deliberately echoes the cloud of glory that filled the tabernacle at its dedication (Exodus 40:34–35) and Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:10–11), and the glory-cloud in Isaiah 6:4. There the priests 'could not stand to minister'; here 'no one could enter,' a sign that the moment for intercession has closed — the plagues will run their full course. The unit is brief and transitional, a hinge between the song (vv.1–4) and the bowls of chapter 16.
5.theological_application: That 'no one could enter the sanctuary until the seven plagues... were finished' (v.8) is a sober word: there is a point at which the door of intercession is shut and judgment is irrevocable. The same passage that began in worship ends with a warning that God's patience, though long, is not endless. Commentators in the Reformed tradition (Matthew Henry, John Gill) press the pastoral edge — that the wrath proceeds from 'God who lives forever' means no oppressor outlasts him, and that the saints' present prayers ascend while the door is open. The application is urgency in prayer and witness now, before the bowls are poured.
5.christology: The sanctuary filled with the glory of God recalls the glory that 'tabernacled' among us in the incarnate Word (John 1:14), and the closed sanctuary stands in pointed contrast to the access Christ opens — for through his flesh believers have 'a new and living way' into the holy places (Hebrews 10:19–20). The bowls fall on those who refused that access. The Lamb who shares the throne (Revelation 5:6) is the one from whose presence the wrath proceeds, so that the crucified mediator is also the righteous judge — the same Christ who is sanctuary and door is also the one whose glory makes the unrepentant unable to stand.
